scenario: Built new copy skill from 3 training docs without checking registry. Created near-duplicate of existing copy_director_v3.
action_taken: Discovered overlap post-build, wasted build cycle. Now enforce registry check as mandatory Phase 1 step.
failure_mode: Building redundant skills wastes cycles and fragments the skill graph
recovery_tactic: Always compare against existing_skill_registry before entering Phase 2. Halt if overlap > 80%.

scenario: Migrated XML skill with 600+ lines. Output skill.md was 520 lines -- still a monolith that exceeded context budget for loading.
trigger: Source XML was monolithic, migration preserved monolithic structure
action_taken: Triggered decompose mode post-migration. Extracted 3 sub-skills, reduced parent to 190 lines.
failure_mode: Monolith skills consume too much context and resist composition
recovery_tactic: If generated skill.md exceeds 400 lines, auto-trigger decompose analysis before finalizing.

scenario: Built skill without declaring phase behavior. MMA could not determine correct scoring weights, defaulted to T3 full evaluation on a T1 research skill.
action_taken: Added phase_type as mandatory field. Skill Builder now requires explicit classification before package completion.
failure_mode: Missing phase declaration causes MMA scoring mismatch
recovery_tactic: Every skill must declare phase_type: exploratory|compositional|executional|resonance-oriented before validation passes.

scenario: Skill had deterministic validation logic embedded as prose instructions in workflow section. Agent spent tokens reasoning about formatting rules that should have been a Python script.
trigger: Deterministic operation described as natural language procedure
action_taken: Extracted validation logic into scripts/validate_output.py. Reduced token usage by ~40% for that workflow step.
failure_mode: Deterministic operations in prose waste tokens and introduce non-determinism
recovery_tactic: During Phase 2, identify any step that has deterministic I/O. Extract to scripts/ as xScript.
